      non-contiguous/partial input
- [x] 1.2 Pin the per-kind merge table (which joins are expressible, which reject and
      why) in the structural-operations delta spec, then implement `mergeNodes` to it
- [x] 1.3 Implement `insertSubtrees`: boundary-only splice, depth re-encoding per the
      existing reparenting rules, typed rejection for inexpressible sequences
- [x] 1.4 Extend the property-test suite to all three ops: closure, totality,
      minimal-edit, gap-accounting round-trips; unit tests for the merge table's
      reject cases and the empty-document edge

## 2. Verdict layer (pure)

- [x] 2.1 Implement `src/enforce.ts`: `(class, edit facts, tree) → pass | rewrite |
      veto`, covering subtree-cover deletion (escalate-then-delete for stale mid-node
      ranges, type-over insertion), single-separator merge recognition, and the
      structural-paste rule (multi-block sequence → insert-at-boundary; single
      block/continuation lines → pass, conservative bias)
- [x] 2.2 Property tests: every rewrite's output re-parses to a well-formed tree with
      no orphans; veto never changes text; within-node edits and all non-enforced
      classes never receive a verdict
- [x] 2.3 Unit tests for the paste heuristic boundary cases (plain multi-line
      fragment, single block, whole-subtree sequence, mixed) and the merge-shape
      recognizer (Backspace-at-start vs Delete-at-end vs multi-char deletions)

## 3. Funnel wiring

- [x] 3.1 Extend `transaction-filter.ts`: hand `boundary-crossing-edit` to the verdict
      layer; rewrites replace the change specs with a plugin-own `userEvent`

      structural-commands cursor contract, single history entry
- [x] 3.2 Implement veto mechanics: dissolve the transaction with no history entry;
      surface the rejection cue via annotation/effect observed outside the filter

- [x] 3.3 Extend `stats.ts` with per-class verdict counters and per-verdict timings;
      per-transaction verdict logging behind the existing crosscheck debug setting

content.text:
## Implementation Complete (pending your manual pass)

**Change:** `outline-edit-enforcement` — **Schema:** spec-driven
**Progress:** 21/21 tasks implemented; 18/21 checked off — the last 3 need your hands in the real vault

### What's done (sections 1–4, fully automated and verified)

- **Pure ops** ([ops.ts](src/ops.ts)): `deleteSubtrees`, `mergeNodes`, `insertSubtrees` — the per-kind merge table is pinned in the [structural-operations spec](openspec/changes/outline-edit-enforcement/specs/structural-operations/spec.md).
- **Verdict layer** ([enforce.ts](src/enforce.ts)): pure `(class, edit, tree) → pass/rewrite/veto`, plus two `classify.ts` extensions it needed (a single-newline boundary deletion and a multi-block paste both otherwise "hide" from the line-span boundary check — documented as real findings, not scope creep).
- **Funnel wiring** ([transaction-filter.ts](src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts)): rewrites replace changes wholesale as one undo step; vetoes dissolve with no history entry and surface a Notice via an update-listener effect (never inside the filter); `stats.ts` gained per-verdict counters.
- **Evidence suite** ([62-outline-edit-enforcement.e2e.ts](e2e/specs/62-outline-edit-enforcement.e2e.ts)): 18 passing scenarios + the find-and-replace automation gap actually closed (it's automatable after all — Phase A never tried); HTML5 drag-drop confirmed still infeasible.
- Caught and fixed two real bugs via the evidence suite before they'd have shipped: a type-over cursor-placement bug and a stale-id lookup after `deleteSubtrees`'s internal reparse (both now have dedicated regression tests).
- Full suite green **twice consecutively**: 720 unit tests, 12/12 e2e spec files (desktop), lint/typecheck clean, mobile-emulation run confirms the skip guards work.
- Findings recorded in [docs/research/04-open-questions.md](docs/research/04-open-questions.md) (Q15) and [design.md](openspec/changes/outline-edit-enforcement/design.md)'s Open Questions.

### What's left — task 5.2's real-vault manual pass

This is subjective-feel judgment I can't substitute for: veto frequency on your own organic editing, whether the paste heuristic ever misfires, how the trailing-gap deletion *feels*, merge ergonomics. The plugin is already built and installed into `test-vault/` (`npm run vault:install` just ran). 

Want me to keep going once you've done that pass (tasks 5.3/5.4 — folding your findings into the docs and amending specs if anything needs tightening), or would you like to try it yourself first and report back?

okay. this is a big change. I need to verify it carefully.

* first, I noticed a bug (might be pre-existing): a paragraph with 1-3 preceeding spaces gets very misaligned, outdented to the leftmost guide line. 
   * the block marker is at the correct relative distance (increases with more spaces), even further to the left. 
   * at 4 spaces it jumps back in place with the 4 spaces shown correctly in the indented block before the text.
   * it can be reproduced by adding a space before any paragraph text line.
* selecting a heading with its subtree, then pressing backspace/delete does nothing and shows a toast "Nothing to act on". also looks like a bug.
   * but strangely selecting two or more sibling headings with their subtrees gets deleted as expected. 
   * I think it goes for any node with children (e.g. paragraph + list, or even list item with a child).
* gap lines are getting in a way of the expected behavior. this has already come up in the selection enforcement implementation, and I feel like it's going to keep coming up until we figure out a better way to deal with the gap lines.
   * right now gap lines are just trailing whitespace owned by the preceeding parsed node/block. but in an outliner there is a difference between the trailing space that is part of a block (in text it would normally be actual indentation whitespace on a separate line, part of the block at the same indentation level), and the empty lines with only a newline character between the blocks (that normally isn't part of the tree).
   * so when I see two sibling nodes and place cursor at the beginning of the second node, and press backspace, I expect that node to get merged with the first one and the text to be appended to the end of it. but because of the gap lines, it doesn't happen like that, nodes get merged, but the text gets appended in place of that gap line. or the gap line gets deleted (e.g. between a heading and a paragraph), but the nodes are not merged. etc.
   * my intuition is that we need to skip over the gap lines and treat them as if they weren't part of the text. they are an implementation detail of the markdown representation which requires or allows to separate blocks with empty lines, but in an outline tree structure, they are not structurally functional and the ownership of those lines is not clear to the user.
   * think about it and let's decide how to deal with this.
* lists are not handled well: if I place cursor at the beginning of any list item and press backspace, I expect it to get merged with the previous list item or its parent paragraph. but instead, it deletes the space after the `-` dash-bullet. turning it into a strange paragraph. 
   * this might be out of scope here, but list markers should not be directly editable in the outline mode. this is what obsidian-outliner tries to do with its "Stick the cursor to the content" functionality. 
* if a list item has a child and I decide to split the parent node by pressing enter in the middle of the text, I expect that to create a new first child (new preceeding sibling to the existing child). but right now it creates a sibling of the parent and it feels very unnatural because it jumps over the child.
   * if the node doesn't have a child, splitting it should create a sibling.
* it seems like there are more quirks and strange behaviors, but I think that we've got enough to work with for now. think about these things as a whole, not just a list of cases to test and fix, but as indicators of the current system not behaving well. consider whether the spec itself needs an adjustment, whether the undelying system needs to change, or whether there is still a gap in our fundamental functionality that prevents us from getting the natural feel of working with an outline. it's probably a combination of all the factors.
Excellent findings — this is exactly the kind of evidence the manual pass exists to produce. Let me first confirm the two mechanical bugs in code, then give you the systemic analysis you asked for.

159	export interface LineGuideFact {

161	  readonly lineNumber: number;

166	   * a list-item chain (deferred entirely to native indent guides, same as
167	   * Experiment 2a's `deep-nesting` fixture result).
168	   */
169	  readonly guideDepths: readonly number[];
170	  /**

172	   * comment above) — these have no corresponding `decorate()` fact at
173	   * all (no depth, no kind), so decorations.ts can't zip this array with

178	}

180	/**

182	 * then children) plus blank trailingGap lines between siblings (see the
183	 * doc comment above) — decorations.ts keys this by `lineNumber`, not by
184	 * array index, since gap lines add entries `decorate()` doesn't have.
185	 */
186	export function computeLineGuides(doc: OutlineDoc): LineGuideFact[] {
187	  const facts: LineGuideFact[] = [];
188	  let current = doc.preamble.length;
189	
190	  const walk = (node: OutlineNode, depth: number, guideDepths: readonly number[]): void => {
191	    for (let i = 0; i < node.lines.length; i++) {
192	      facts.push({ lineNumber: current + i, guideDepths, isGapLine: false });
193	    }
194	    current += node.lines.length;
195	
196	    // This node starts owning a guide for its own children from here on —
197	    // unless it's a list item, which never owns one (see doc comment above).
198	    const childGuideDepths = node.kind === 'list-item' ? guideDepths : [...guideDepths, depth];
199	
200	    // Every trailing gap gets a fact now, for full continuity (see the doc
201	    // comment above): a leaf's own gap uses its own guideDepths; a node

input.title: Fix decoration misalignment for space-indented paragraphs
input.prompt:
In /Users/laughedelic/Code/obsidian-true-outliner: a paragraph line with 1-3 leading spaces renders badly misaligned in outline mode — outdented to the leftmost guide line, with its block marker even further left (the marker's offset grows with the number of spaces). At 4 leading spaces it snaps back into place with the spaces rendered inside the indented block. Reproduce by adding a single space before any paragraph text line in an outline-mode note.

Diagnosis starting points: src/plugin/decorate.ts assumes headings/paragraphs "have no native indentation of their own" and decorations.ts sets padding-left/margin-left = depth × unit; but Obsidian natively styles leading-whitespace lines (HyperMD hang: text-indent/padding-left pair), and at exactly 4 spaces stock Obsidian switches to indented-code-block styling — our parser (src/parse.ts) deliberately treats top-level 4-space-indented lines as paragraphs, which explains the threshold behavior. The MarginCompensation ViewPlugin in src/plugin/decorations.ts patches margin-left inline with !important and may be clobbering or mis-composing with the native hang for 1-3-space paragraph lines. Verify against stock (outline mode off) rendering first, write a failing e2e assertion in the 50-decorations family, then fix so space-indented paragraphs align identically to their unindented siblings plus their own literal whitespace. This bug pre-dates the outline-edit-enforcement change (found 2026-07-21 during its manual pass).
input.tldr: Investigate and fix a pre-existing rendering bug where paragraphs starting with one to three spaces get visually pushed far left in outline mode, with their block markers drifting even further.

content.text:
All green — 722 unit tests, 19 e2e scenarios including the new regressions. Here's the full picture.

## Status of your five findings

1. **1-3-space paragraph misalignment** — confirmed pre-existing (no decoration file is touched by this change). Mechanism: `decorate.ts` assumes paragraphs have no native indentation, but Obsidian natively styles leading-whitespace lines with its own hang, and at exactly 4 spaces stock Obsidian switches to indented-code styling — hence the "snaps back" threshold. I filed it as a separate task chip so it doesn't contaminate this change.
2. **Heading+subtree Backspace → "Nothing to act on"** — real bug in this change, **fixed**. The cover math missed the case where the selection's start node is an *ancestor* of its end node (one subtree selected): the sibling-run computation indexed past the ancestor's path, produced an empty cover, and `deleteSubtrees([])` vetoed. Sibling selections diverge earlier in the path, which is why two headings worked. `escalate.ts` already handled this case; `enforce.ts` now mirrors it, with unit regressions for heading, paragraph+list, and nested-list shapes plus an e2e scenario.
3. **Gap lines fighting merges** — systemic, analysis below.
4. **List-item Backspace eating the marker space** — same root cause, below.
5. **Split jumping over children** — spec'd behavior (`structural-operations` "Node split": *children stay with the upper node*; `outline-keyboard-grammar`: *splits into two siblings*), so changing it is a spec amendment, also below.

## The systemic diagnosis

Findings 3, 4, and 5 are one gap, not three. The system operates in two coordinate spaces that don't agree:

- **Markdown character space** — what the funnel sees: raw positions, gap lines, list markers, indentation.
- **Outline content space** — what you think in: nodes with content, adjacency between contents, chrome invisible.

The verdict layer recognizes intent from raw edit *shapes*. That works where the two spaces coincide (whole-subtree deletions, multi-block pastes) and misfires exactly where they diverge — which is everywhere chrome sits between the cursor and the content boundary:

- **Gaps** (chrome *between* nodes): Backspace at a node's start with a gap above deletes a gap newline. In character space that's innocuous whitespace editing, so it passes natively — producing your "text lands where the gap was" weirdness, one keystroke at a time.
- **Markers** (chrome *inside* nodes): Backspace at a list item's content start deletes the marker's space. In character space that's a within-node edit, so it passes — producing the `-item` garbage paragraph.
- **Split**: `splitNode` thinks in encoding terms (the remainder continues at the same depth → sibling), but content adjacency says the new node belongs right after the split point — which, when children exist, is *before* them: the first child.

Your instinct about the model is right in a specific way: trailing-gap ownership is correct for **storage** (byte fidelity needs every line owned by something), but it was never supposed to become a **user-facing semantic**. Phase B dodged this locally (gap-line trigger, content-end covers); Phase C ran into it head-on; cursor movement and gap rendering will hit it next. I don't think the tree model needs to change — what's missing is a declared invariant:

> **Chrome transparency**: gap lines and structural markers are encoding chrome. No user-facing editing semantic may depend on gap width, gap ownership, or marker internals. Content-level intents are interpreted in content space; the system maintains the chrome. *Escape hatch*: a cursor placed **on** a gap line editing the gap itself stays native — that's deliberate whitespace authoring.

## What that means concretely

**A. Chrome-transparent merges** (amends this change's merge requirement, replacing the "single-separator deletion" framing):

- Backspace with the cursor at a node's **first content character** — deleting backward into any chrome (separator newline, gap line, marker space) — is a merge request: merge this node into its content-space predecessor, **consuming the whole gap**, text appended directly to the end of the predecessor's content (`First.Second.`, one undo step). Delete at a node's **last content character** mirrors it.
- Disambiguation needs one new fact: the pre-edit cursor. Backspace-at-node-start and Delete-at-end-of-gap-line produce byte-identical transactions; only the cursor position tells "merge intent" from "shrinking the gap on purpose." The filter already has `tr.startState.selection` — this stays input-agnostic (any gesture producing the same edit from the same cursor is treated identically).
- Revised merge table: content kinds join **across** kinds (list-item text into a paragraph and vice versa — result keeps the survivor's kind and marker, matching your "merged with the previous list item or its parent paragraph"); children never orphan — they re-parent into the merged node at the absorbed node's position; absorbing a *heading* still vetoes; atoms still veto.
- This supersedes the "two-Backspace native join" e2e finding — the first Backspace becomes the enforced merge.

**B. Split-with-children** — amend `structural-operations` + `outline-keyboard-grammar`: node has children → remainder becomes the **first child** (encoded per the child scope's kind rules); no children → sibling, as today.

**C. Full marker protection** (cursor can't enter the marker region, à la obsidian-outliner's "stick to content") — that's cursor/selection UX, Track 2 in docs/research/13, its own change. The *edit* half you actually hit (backspace-at-content-start) is covered by A.

Two judgment calls I can't settle alone:

questions.question: Backspace at the start of the first paragraph under a heading (gap-transparent, so it reaches the heading directly): should it merge the paragraph's text into the heading line, or veto with the rejection cue? Outliners like Logseq merge (a heading is just a node); markdown headings are single-line, so only single-line content could ever join — multi-line content would veto either way.

options.label: Merge single-line, veto rest (Recommended)
options.description: Single-line paragraph text appends to the heading title (one undo step); multi-line content and anything inexpressible vetoes with the cue. Matches outliner intuition and avoids 'my key does nothing' friction on a very common keystroke.

options.label: Always veto
options.description: Backspace into a heading never merges — document unchanged, transient cue explains why. More conservative: heading titles never change by accident, at the cost of a dead-feeling key at every section's first paragraph.

questions.question: Where should these amendments be implemented? Both reshape rules this change owns (merge semantics) or touches (split is in two existing capabilities' specs).

options.label: All in this change (Recommended)
options.description: Amend the delta specs per the task 5.4 / Phase A+B precedent: chrome-transparent merges + revised merge table in node-edit-enforcement/structural-operations deltas, plus new deltas for the split rule. One coherent 'edits feel like an outline' change; verification restarts from the amended specs.

options.label: Merges here, split separately
options.description: Merge semantics amend this change (they're its own requirement); the split-with-children rule becomes a small follow-up change against structural-operations + outline-keyboard-grammar. Cleaner capability boundaries, one more change to shepherd.

### Requirement: Enter splits the node
In outline mode, Enter SHALL split the node at the cursor into two siblings of the same
kind (empty lower half when the cursor is at the node's end), placing the cursor at the
new sibling's content start. On a heading line, Enter SHALL instead create an empty
paragraph as the heading's first child. On an atom's interior, Enter SHALL decline the
key (stock newline).

#### Scenario: Split a list item mid-text
- **WHEN** Enter is pressed with the cursor inside `- alpha beta`, after "alpha "
- **THEN** the text becomes two sibling items `- alpha ` and `- beta` (children, if any,
  staying with the first) and the cursor sits after the new item's marker

#### Scenario: Enter at end creates an empty sibling
- **WHEN** Enter is pressed at the end of a list item's text
- **THEN** a new empty sibling item appears below and the cursor sits on it

### Requirement: Shift+Enter continues the node
In outline mode, Shift+Enter SHALL insert a newline that keeps the cursor inside the SAME
node as a continuation line — indented to the content column for list items, a plain
=== e2e-verification ===
### Requirement: Keyboard grammar e2e verification

The harness SHALL verify the outline keyboard grammar with real key events:
Tab/Shift+Tab indent/outdent at the cursor node, Alt+Up/Down move nodes,
Enter splits per node kind, Shift+Enter continues an item as one node, atom
interiors behave stock, keys behave stock when mode is off, and a mode
toggle takes effect on the very next keypress. Each accepted grammar
operation MUST be one undo step; each rejected one MUST change nothing but
show its cue.

#### Scenario: Off-mode keys are stock

- **WHEN** Tab, Enter, Shift+Enter, and Alt+arrows are pressed in a list in
  a non-outline note
- **THEN** the buffer changes match stock Obsidian behavior (no grammar
  transforms, no notices)

#### Scenario: Toggle applies to the next keypress

- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled while the note is open and a grammar key
  is pressed immediately after
- **THEN** the keypress follows the new mode

#### Scenario: Tab family and moves

- **WHEN** Tab / Shift+Tab / Alt+Up / Alt+Down are pressed at a node
- **THEN** the node indents/outdents/moves per the grammar, the cursor lands
  at content start (for indents), and ordered runs renumber

#### Scenario: Enter split semantics per kind

- **WHEN** Enter is pressed mid-item, at item end, at paragraph end, and on
  a heading
- **THEN** respectively: the item splits with children staying up; an empty
  `- ` sibling appears with the cursor after the marker; a blank line plus
  cursor appears and typed text becomes the sibling; an empty line appears
  below the heading and typed text becomes a child paragraph

#### Scenario: Shift+Enter keeps one node

- **WHEN** Shift+Enter is pressed inside an item and a structural op then
=== structural-ops main split ===
- **THEN** either it is rejected, or `parse(encode(result.tree))` equals `result.tree` and
  applying `result.edits` to the source text yields `encode(result.tree)`

### Requirement: Node split
`splitNode(doc, nodeId, position)` SHALL split a paragraph or list-item node at a
document position within its text into two adjacent siblings of the same kind: the
original node keeps the text before the position (and all children); a new sibling of
the same kind holds the text after it (empty if the position is at the node's end). List
items reuse the original's marker style (ordered runs renumber); paragraphs gain the
separating blank line the boundary rules require. Headings and atoms SHALL be rejected
with `cannot-split`. The operation SHALL satisfy the same contract as all structural
operations: typed rejection or `{tree, edits, cursor}` where the result re-parses
identically from its own encoding, edits reproduce the encoding, untouched nodes keep
verbatim lines, and `cursor` points at the new sibling's content start.

#### Scenario: Mid-text split of a list item
- **WHEN** `- alpha beta` is split after `alpha `
- **THEN** the encoding contains sibling items `- alpha ` and `- beta`, any children
  remain under the first, and re-parsing yields exactly that tree

#### Scenario: End-of-node split
- **WHEN** a node is split at the exact end of its text
- **THEN** for a list item the new sibling is an empty item node (`- `) with the cursor
  after its marker; for a paragraph — whose empty form has no markdown encoding — the
  split yields only the blank separation with the cursor on it, and the sibling node
  materializes when text is typed

#### Scenario: Atom split rejected
- **WHEN** splitting is attempted at a position inside a code fence
- **THEN** the operation is rejected with `cannot-split` and nothing changes

content.text: Now the artifact amendments. First the node-edit-enforcement delta — the chrome-transparency principle and the content-adjacent merge requirement:

input.old_string:
### Requirement: Boundary Backspace and Delete become merges or vetoes
A single-separator deletion joining two nodes — Backspace at a node's first character
or Delete at a node's last character — SHALL be rewritten to the structural merge of
the two nodes when the merge is expressible under the per-kind algebra, and SHALL be
vetoed when it is not (including any merge that would re-parent or orphan children).
The behavior SHALL derive from the edit's shape, not from key bindings, so any
gesture producing the same edit is enforced identically.

#### Scenario: Paragraph joins its predecessor
- **WHEN** the cursor is at the first character of a paragraph whose previous sibling
  is a paragraph and the user presses Backspace
- **THEN** the two paragraphs merge into one node as a single structural edit

#### Scenario: Structure-corrupting merge is vetoed
- **WHEN** the user presses Backspace at the start of a heading whose section
  contains child nodes, and the resulting join is inexpressible without re-parenting
  those children
- **THEN** the document is unchanged and the rejection cue is shown
input.new_string:
### Requirement: Editing semantics are chrome-transparent
Gap lines and structural markers are encoding chrome: no user-facing editing
semantic SHALL depend on gap width, gap ownership, or marker internals. An edit
expressing a content-level intent SHALL be interpreted in content space — the space
of node contents and the boundaries between them — with the chrome maintained by the
system. Exception (the deliberate authoring escape hatch): an edit made with the
cursor placed ON a gap line, operating on the gap itself, SHALL stay native.

*(Amendment 2026-07-21, real-vault manual pass: the original single-separator merge
rule made every merge require first manually deleting the gap, one newline per
keystroke, with confusing intermediate states — gap ownership leaking into editing
semantics. This requirement pins the general principle; the merge requirement below
is its first application.)*

#### Scenario: Gap width never changes merge behavior
- **WHEN** the user presses Backspace at a node's first content character, with zero,
  one, or three blank lines separating it from the previous node
- **THEN** the outcome is identical in all three cases — the merge (or its veto)
  behaves as if the gap did not exist, and an accepted merge consumes the gap whole

#### Scenario: Editing the gap itself stays native
- **WHEN** the user places the cursor on a blank gap line and presses Backspace or
  Delete to shrink the gap
- **THEN** the edit applies exactly as stock — deliberate whitespace authoring is
  never rewritten

### Requirement: Content-adjacent deletions become merges or vetoes
A deletion expressing "join this node with its content-space neighbor" SHALL be
rewritten to the structural merge of the two nodes when the merge is expressible
under the per-kind algebra, and SHALL be vetoed with the rejection cue when it is
not. The recognized shapes, all cursor-derived and input-agnostic (any gesture
producing the same edit from the same cursor position is enforced identically):

- Backspace with the cursor at a node's first content character — deleting backward
  into chrome (the separator newline, a gap line's newline, or a list marker's
  trailing space) — merges that node into its content-space predecessor (the node
  whose content ends nearest above; possibly its parent or a previous sibling's
  deepest descendant).
- Delete with the cursor at a node's last content character — deleting forward into
  chrome — merges the node's content-space successor into it. When no successor
  exists, the edit passes natively (trailing whitespace editing, nothing structural
  below).
- A node with no content-space predecessor (the document's first node) vetoes its
  Backspace-merge with the cue rather than passing a chrome-corrupting deletion.

An accepted merge SHALL append the absorbed node's content directly to the end of
the surviving node's content (no continuation-line remnant standing in for the old
gap), consume the intervening gap entirely, re-parent the absorbed node's children
per the algebra, and form one undo step.

#### Scenario: Paragraph joins across a gap in one keystroke
- **WHEN** the cursor is at the first character of a paragraph separated from the
  previous paragraph by a blank line and the user presses Backspace
- **THEN** the two paragraphs merge into one node with the second's text appended
  directly after the first's, the gap gone, as a single structural edit

#### Scenario: List item merges into its parent paragraph
- **WHEN** the cursor is at the content start of the first list item under a
  paragraph (after the `- ` marker) and the user presses Backspace
- **THEN** the item's text is appended to the paragraph's text, the item's children
  re-parent under the merged node, and no marker fragment is left behind

#### Scenario: Structure-corrupting merge is vetoed
- **WHEN** the user presses Backspace at the first character of a heading (a merge
  that would absorb the heading and destroy its section's anchor)
- **THEN** the document is unchanged and the rejection cue is shown

### Requirement: Adjacent-node merge
A `mergeNodes` operation SHALL join a node with its immediately following neighbor
under a per-kind algebra: joins that concatenate content within the same structural
shape (paragraph←paragraph, list-item←list-item of compatible markers) SHALL be
performed as minimal edits; joins that would re-parent or orphan either node's
children, absorb a heading (and thereby its section), or produce markdown that
re-parses to a different structure than the merged tree SHALL be rejected with a
typed reason.

"Immediately following neighbor" is the node's document-order successor: its own
first child if it has one, else its next sibling, else the nearest ancestor's next
sibling (`rawSuccessorPath`) — the same node Delete-at-end abuts in the raw buffer,
and (walked from a Backspace-at-start predecessor) the same node Backspace-at-start
abuts too. One primitive serves both gestures.

Preconditions checked before the kind table (either failing rejects regardless of
kind): either side having children rejects with `would-orphan-children` (a node about
to disappear into its neighbor has no valid re-parenting target for its own
children); no following neighbor at all (last node in the document) rejects with
`no-following-neighbor`.

The per-kind merge table, pinned by implementation and exercised by the property
suite:

| First ＼ Second | paragraph | list-item | heading | atom |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **paragraph** | join: concatenate text, second's first line becomes the tail of first's last line | reject `merge-not-expressible` — cross-kind join has no positional encoding | reject `merge-not-expressible` (children present: `would-orphan-children`) — absorbing a heading destroys its section's positional anchor | reject `merge-not-expressible` — atoms are opaque units |
| **list-item** | reject `merge-not-expressible` | join if same list-style family (bullet↔bullet or ordered↔ordered — marker character need not match, the surviving node keeps its own); reject `merge-not-expressible` across families | reject `merge-not-expressible` (or `would-orphan-children`) | reject `merge-not-expressible` |
| **heading** | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` |
| **atom** | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` |

A heading is never `first` under the Backspace/Delete gesture mapping (its own
predecessor plays that role instead — see node-edit-enforcement's boundary-merge
requirement); the row exists for `mergeNodes`'s own totality, exercised directly by
the property suite.

#### Scenario: Paragraph merge is a minimal edit
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` joins two adjacent sibling paragraphs
- **THEN** the result is one paragraph node whose text is the concatenation, all
  other lines byte-identical

#### Scenario: Merge that would orphan children is rejected
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` would join a node into a predecessor such that the node's
  children could not keep a valid parent in the encoded markdown
- **THEN** the operation returns a rejection and the document is unchanged
input.new_string:
### Requirement: Adjacent-node merge
A `mergeNodes` operation SHALL join a node (`first`) with its immediately following
content-space neighbor (`second`) under a per-kind algebra, appending `second`'s
content directly to the end of `first`'s content — never leaving a continuation-line
remnant standing where the old separation was — consuming `first`'s trailing gap,
and re-parenting `second`'s children under the merged node. Joins that would absorb
a heading (and thereby its section's positional anchor), involve an atom on either
side, or produce markdown that re-parses to a different structure than the merged
tree SHALL be rejected with a typed reason.

*(Amended 2026-07-21 from the original conservative table, per the real-vault manual
pass: cross-kind content joins ARE the expected behavior — a list item's text merges
into its parent paragraph — and children re-parent rather than reject, matching
content-space outliner semantics. See node-edit-enforcement's chrome-transparency
requirement.)*

"Immediately following content-space neighbor" is the node's document-order
successor: its own first child if it has one, else its next sibling, else the
nearest ancestor's next sibling (`rawSuccessorPath`) — the same node whose content
begins nearest below `first`'s content end, regardless of intervening gap lines.

Preconditions checked before the kind table: no following neighbor at all (last
node in the document) rejects with `no-following-neighbor`.

The per-kind merge table (rows = `first`, columns = `second`), pinned by
implementation and exercised by the property suite:

| First ＼ Second | paragraph / list-item | heading | atom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **paragraph / list-item** | join: `second`'s first content line (marker stripped) appends to `first`'s last content line; `second`'s continuation lines become `first`-kind continuations; `first` keeps its own kind and marker; `second`'s children re-parent under the merged node at `second`'s former position, re-encoded for the new scope | reject `merge-not-expressible` — absorbing a heading destroys its section's positional anchor | reject `merge-not-expressible` — atoms are opaque units |
| **heading** | join iff `second`'s content is a single line: it appends to the heading's text line, and `second`'s children re-parent as section children; multi-line content rejects `merge-not-expressible` (a markdown heading cannot hold continuation lines) | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` |
| **atom** | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` | reject `merge-not-expressible` |

#### Scenario: Paragraph merge appends at content end
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` joins two paragraphs separated by a blank gap line
- **THEN** the result is one paragraph node whose last content line is the direct
  concatenation of the two texts, the gap is gone, and all other lines are
  byte-identical

#### Scenario: Cross-kind join keeps the survivor's encoding
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` joins a paragraph with its first child list item
- **THEN** the item's text (marker stripped) appends to the paragraph's text, the
  merged node stays a paragraph, and the item's children re-parent under it

#### Scenario: Children re-parent instead of rejecting
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` absorbs a node that has children of its own
- **THEN** those children keep their order and relative structure under the merged
  node, re-encoded for the new scope, and the result re-parses to exactly that tree

#### Scenario: Single-line content joins a heading
- **WHEN** `mergeNodes` joins a heading with a following single-line paragraph
- **THEN** the paragraph's text appends to the heading's title line; a multi-line
  paragraph in the same position is rejected with `merge-not-expressible`

### Requirement: Node split lands content-adjacent
`splitNode` on a node WITH children SHALL place the remainder as the node's new
FIRST CHILD — the position content-adjacent to the split point — encoded per the
child scope's kind rules (a paragraph parent's new child becomes a list item when
its siblings are list items, per the attachment rule). A node with NO children keeps
the existing behavior: the remainder becomes the next sibling of the same kind. The
cursor contract (remainder's content start) and the operation guarantees (closure,
minimal edits, typed rejection) are unchanged.

*(Amended 2026-07-21: the original children-stay-up sibling split made the new node
visually jump over the whole subtree — unnatural in content space. Modifies the main
spec's "Node split" requirement.)*

#### Scenario: Splitting a parent puts the remainder before the children
- **WHEN** `- alpha beta` with a child `- gamma` is split after `alpha `
- **THEN** the tree is `- alpha ` with children `- beta`, `- gamma` — the remainder
  is the first child, not a sibling below `- gamma`

#### Scenario: Childless split is unchanged
- **WHEN** a childless `- alpha beta` is split after `alpha `
- **THEN** the result is the sibling pair `- alpha ` / `- beta`, exactly as before

## MODIFIED Requirements

### Requirement: Node split
`splitNode(doc, nodeId, position)` SHALL split a paragraph or list-item node at a
document position within its text. For a node WITH children, the remainder SHALL
become the node's new FIRST CHILD — the position content-adjacent to the split point
— encoded per the child scope's kind rules (a paragraph parent's new child becomes a
list item when its existing children are list items, per the attachment rule). For a
node with NO children, the remainder becomes the next sibling of the same kind, as
before: list items reuse the original's marker style (ordered runs renumber);
paragraphs gain the separating blank line the boundary rules require; an
end-of-paragraph split yields only the blank separation with the cursor on it (an
empty paragraph has no markdown encoding). Headings and atoms SHALL be rejected with
`cannot-split`. The operation SHALL satisfy the same contract as all structural
operations: typed rejection or `{tree, edits, cursor}` where the result re-parses
identically from its own encoding, edits reproduce the encoding, untouched nodes
keep verbatim lines, and `cursor` points at the remainder's content start.

*(Amended 2026-07-21, real-vault manual pass: the original children-stay-up sibling
split made the new node visually jump over the whole subtree — unnatural in content
space.)*

#### Scenario: Splitting a parent puts the remainder before the children
- **WHEN** `- alpha beta` with a child `- gamma` is split after `alpha `
- **THEN** the tree is `- alpha ` with children `- beta` then `- gamma` — the
  remainder is the first child, not a sibling below the subtree

#### Scenario: Childless split is unchanged
- **WHEN** a childless `- alpha beta` is split after `alpha `
- **THEN** the encoding contains sibling items `- alpha ` and `- beta`, and
  re-parsing yields exactly that tree

#### Scenario: Atom split rejected
- **WHEN** splitting is attempted at a position inside a code fence
- **THEN** the operation is rejected with `cannot-split` and nothing changes

input.old_string:
### Requirement: Every transaction receives exactly one classification
In outline-mode editors the filter SHALL assign each transaction exactly one class from:
`programmatic`, `composition`, `plugin-own`, `selection-only`, `within-node-edit`,
`boundary-crossing-edit` — evaluated in that order, first match wins. Classification
SHALL be computed by a pure function over transaction facts and the parsed tree, unit-
and property-tested independently of Obsidian. Any transaction not confidently matching
an enforced class SHALL pass through unmodified (default-permit). Transactions
classified `boundary-crossing-edit` SHALL additionally be handed to the
node-edit-enforcement verdict layer, which determines whether they pass, are
rewritten, or are vetoed.

#### Scenario: Typing inside a node
- **WHEN** the user types a character in the middle of a paragraph node's text
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `within-node-edit` and applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Edit spanning two nodes is counted and enforced
- **WHEN** a deletion's change range starts inside one node and ends inside the next
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `boundary-crossing-edit`, counted in the stats
  surface, and receives a verdict per the node-edit-enforcement capability
input.new_string:
### Requirement: Every transaction receives exactly one classification
In outline-mode editors the filter SHALL assign each transaction exactly one class from:
`programmatic`, `composition`, `plugin-own`, `selection-only`, `within-node-edit`,
`boundary-crossing-edit` — evaluated in that order, first match wins. Classification
SHALL be computed by a pure function over transaction facts and the parsed tree, unit-
and property-tested independently of Obsidian. Any transaction not confidently matching
an enforced class SHALL pass through unmodified (default-permit). Transactions
classified `boundary-crossing-edit` SHALL additionally be handed to the
node-edit-enforcement verdict layer, which determines whether they pass, are
rewritten, or are vetoed.

`boundary-crossing-edit` covers, beyond change ranges whose line spans touch more than
one node: pure insertions whose inserted text parses as a multi-block sequence
(landing on a node's own line), single-character deletions of a line boundary whose
adjacent lines belong to different nodes, and — per node-edit-enforcement's
chrome-transparency requirement (amendment 2026-07-21) — chrome-boundary deletions
whose merge intent is established by the pre-edit cursor position: a deletion of a
list marker's trailing space ending exactly at the item's first content column with
the cursor there, and a deletion of the newline ending a node's last content line
with the cursor at that node's content end (Delete into the node's own trailing
gap). The pre-edit main-selection cursor is a classification fact supplied by the
adapter for exactly these shapes; an edit with the same bytes but a different cursor
(editing the gap from within it) remains `within-node-edit`.

#### Scenario: Typing inside a node
- **WHEN** the user types a character in the middle of a paragraph node's text
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `within-node-edit` and applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Edit spanning two nodes is counted and enforced
- **WHEN** a deletion's change range starts inside one node and ends inside the next
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `boundary-crossing-edit`, counted in the stats
  surface, and receives a verdict per the node-edit-enforcement capability

#### Scenario: Marker-space deletion at content start is enforced
- **WHEN** the cursor sits at a list item's first content character and Backspace
  deletes the marker's trailing space
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `boundary-crossing-edit` and handed to the
  verdict layer (a merge intent), not applied as a within-node marker corruption

#### Scenario: The same bytes with a gap-line cursor stay native
- **WHEN** a deletion removes the newline between a node's last content line and its
  own trailing gap, with the pre-edit cursor ON the gap line
- **THEN** the transaction is classified `within-node-edit` and applied unmodified
  (deliberate whitespace authoring)

# outline-keyboard-grammar Delta

*(Added by amendment 2026-07-21, real-vault manual pass — see design.md's
amendment log and structural-operations' modified "Node split" requirement.)*

## MODIFIED Requirements

### Requirement: Enter splits the node
In outline mode, Enter SHALL split the node at the cursor. For a node WITH
children, the remainder becomes the node's new FIRST CHILD — content-adjacent to
the split point, never jumping over the existing subtree — encoded per the child
scope's kind rules. For a node with NO children, the remainder becomes a sibling of
the same kind (empty lower half when the cursor is at the node's end), as before.
The cursor lands at the remainder's content start. On a heading line, Enter SHALL
instead create an empty paragraph as the heading's first child. On an atom's
interior, Enter SHALL decline the key (stock newline).

#### Scenario: Split a childless list item mid-text
- **WHEN** Enter is pressed with the cursor inside a childless `- alpha beta`,
  after "alpha "
- **THEN** the text becomes two sibling items `- alpha ` and `- beta` and the
  cursor sits after the new item's marker

#### Scenario: Split a parent lands the remainder as first child
- **WHEN** Enter is pressed mid-text in a list item that has children
- **THEN** the remainder becomes the item's new first child, sitting directly
  below the split point and above the existing children

#### Scenario: Enter at end creates an empty sibling
- **WHEN** Enter is pressed at the end of a childless list item's text
- **THEN** a new empty sibling item appears below and the cursor sits on it

# e2e-verification Delta

*(Added by amendment 2026-07-21, real-vault manual pass — keeps the harness
requirement's split-scenario text consistent with the modified split semantics.)*

## MODIFIED Requirements

### Requirement: Keyboard grammar e2e verification

The harness SHALL verify the outline keyboard grammar with real key events:
Tab/Shift+Tab indent/outdent at the cursor node, Alt+Up/Down move nodes,
Enter splits per node kind, Shift+Enter continues an item as one node, atom
interiors behave stock, keys behave stock when mode is off, and a mode
toggle takes effect on the very next keypress. Each accepted grammar
operation MUST be one undo step; each rejected one MUST change nothing but
show its cue.

#### Scenario: Off-mode keys are stock

- **WHEN** Tab, Enter, Shift+Enter, and Alt+arrows are pressed in a list in
  a non-outline note
- **THEN** the buffer changes match stock Obsidian behavior (no grammar
  transforms, no notices)

#### Scenario: Toggle applies to the next keypress

- **WHEN** outline mode is toggled while the note is open and a grammar key
  is pressed immediately after
- **THEN** the keypress follows the new mode

#### Scenario: Tab family and moves

- **WHEN** Tab / Shift+Tab / Alt+Up / Alt+Down are pressed at a node
- **THEN** the node indents/outdents/moves per the grammar, the cursor lands
  at content start (for indents), and ordered runs renumber

#### Scenario: Enter split semantics per kind

- **WHEN** Enter is pressed mid-item (childless), mid-item (with children), at
  item end, at paragraph end, and on a heading
- **THEN** respectively: the childless item splits into siblings; the parent
  item's remainder becomes its new first child above the existing children; an
  empty `- ` sibling appears with the cursor after the marker; a blank line plus
  cursor appears and typed text becomes the sibling; an empty line appears
  below the heading and typed text becomes a child paragraph

#### Scenario: Shift+Enter keeps one node

- **WHEN** Shift+Enter is pressed inside an item and a structural op then
  targets it
- **THEN** the continuation line moves with the item as one node

input.old_string: ## Risks / Trade-offs
input.new_string:
### D9. Chrome transparency (amendment 2026-07-21, real-vault manual pass)

The first manual pass surfaced one systemic failure mode behind three symptoms
(gap-blocked merges, marker-space Backspace corruption, split jumping over
children): the verdict layer recognized intents in **markdown character space**
while the user acts in **outline content space** — and everywhere chrome (gap
lines, list markers) sits between the cursor and the content boundary, the two
diverge. The model's trailing-gap ownership is correct for STORAGE (byte fidelity)
but was leaking into user-facing semantics. Adopted principle (pinned as a
node-edit-enforcement requirement): gap lines and structural markers are encoding
chrome; no editing semantic may depend on gap width, gap ownership, or marker
internals. Escape hatch: a cursor placed ON a gap line editing the gap stays
native.

### D10. Content-adjacent merges (amendment 2026-07-21)

Replaces D4's single-separator recognition: Backspace with the cursor at a node's
first content character (deleting into any chrome — separator newline, gap
newline, marker space) merges the node into its content-space predecessor,
consuming the whole gap; Delete at a node's last content character mirrors it.
Recognition is cursor-derived (the pre-edit main selection becomes a
classification/enforcement fact — Backspace-at-node-start and Delete-at-end-of-gap
produce byte-identical transactions, only the cursor distinguishes merge intent
from deliberate gap editing) but stays input-agnostic across gestures. The merge
table is re-pinned: content kinds join across kinds (survivor keeps its own kind
and marker; absorbed content appends directly at the survivor's content end — no
continuation-line remnant); children re-parent instead of rejecting; a heading
absorbs single-line content only (per the manual-pass decision); absorbing a
heading, and atoms on either side, still veto. First-node Backspace-merge vetoes
with the cue rather than passing a chrome-corrupting deletion.

### D11. Split lands content-adjacent (amendment 2026-07-21)

`splitNode` on a node with children places the remainder as the new FIRST CHILD
(encoded per the child scope's kind rules) instead of a sibling beyond the whole
subtree; childless splits are unchanged. Modifies structural-operations' "Node
split" and outline-keyboard-grammar's "Enter splits the node" (deltas added to
this change per the A+B amendment precedent).

## Risks / Trade-offs

## Q16. First real-vault manual pass — chrome-transparency amendment ✅ RESOLVED (2026-07-21)

The first real-vault pass of `outline-edit-enforcement` surfaced four symptoms that
turned out to be one systemic gap, not four independent bugs:

- A paragraph selection with a child list, or a selected heading with its own
  subtree, hit "Nothing to act on" on Backspace/Delete — a genuine implementation bug
  (below), not the systemic issue.
- Backspace at a node's first character did nothing useful across a real blank-line
  gap — it took one keystroke per gap line before anything merged.
- Backspace at a list item's content start ate the marker's trailing space instead of
  merging, corrupting the item into a stray paragraph fragment.
- Enter mid-text in a node WITH children created the remainder as a sibling PAST the
  whole subtree — visually jumping over the children instead of landing next to the
  split point.

**Diagnosis**: the verdict layer recognized intent from raw markdown-character-space
edit shapes, while the user acts in outline-content-space (nodes and the adjacency
between their CONTENTS, chrome invisible). The two coincide for whole-subtree
deletions and multi-block pastes (why those worked correctly in evidence-suite
testing) and diverge everywhere chrome — gap lines, list markers — sits between the
cursor and the content boundary. Trailing-gap ownership is correct for STORAGE (byte
fidelity) but had leaked into user-facing EDITING semantics — exactly the shape this
series' own document (docs/research/13) flagged as a recurring theme across
selection enforcement and now edit enforcement.

**Resolution** — a new principle (chrome transparency, D9) plus two rule amendments,
implemented and re-verified in the same session (all deltas amended, code revised,
full suite green twice, mobile-emulation confirmed):

- **D10, content-adjacent merges**: recognition is now cursor-derived, not just
  edit-shape-derived — the pre-edit main-selection position distinguishes "Backspace
  at a node's content start reaching into chrome" (merge intent, whatever the gap
  width) from "cursor left on a blank gap line, editing the gap" (stays native — the
  deliberate escape hatch). The merge table gained cross-kind joins (list item into
  its parent paragraph and vice versa — the common real case), children re-parenting
  instead of rejecting, and single-line heading absorption (a markdown heading has no
  continuation lines, so multi-line content still rejects).
- **D11, content-adjacent split**: `splitNode` on a node WITH children now lands the
  remainder as the new FIRST CHILD, matching where the split point actually is.

**A separate, real implementation bug** (not the chrome-transparency issue, though
surfaced by the same test): the deletion cover computation
(`siblingCoverIds`/`enforce.ts`) mishandled the case where one end of a range is an
ANCESTOR of the other (a single node selected together with its own subtree) —
escalate.ts's own scope resolution already had this fallback (the scope is one level
above the shallower node), `enforce.ts` didn't mirror it, so the cover came back
empty and the deletion vetoed as "Nothing to act on." Sibling-subtree selections
(paths diverging before either ends) masked it in prior testing. Fixed with
unit + e2e regressions.

**Harness note**: a mobile-emulation-only failure surfaced in the direct-CM6-dispatch
selection helper (`dispatchSelectOnlyRanges`) immediately followed by a keypress —
the dispatched selection didn't survive to the Backspace event under mobile Chrome
emulation specifically (`Editor.setSelection` immediately followed by a keypress
works fine on both platforms). Not a product bug; the affected e2e scenario now uses
`setSelection` instead, which is also a MORE representative test of the D3
stale-selection path.

**Not filed here**: a pre-existing decoration bug (paragraphs with 1–3 leading spaces
render badly misaligned) was also found during this pass. It touches no file this
change owns and was spun off as its own task, not folded into node-edit-enforcement's
scope.
EOF

input.content:
---
name: chrome-transparency-editing
description: editing semantics must never depend on gap-line/marker chrome; recognize intent from cursor position, not raw edit shape
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

When implementing an editing/enforcement rule on top of the block-tree model, check whether the rule is being recognized in **markdown character space** (raw line spans, gap lines, list markers) versus **outline content space** (node contents and the adjacency between them, chrome invisible). The two coincide for whole-subtree operations but diverge anywhere chrome — trailing gap lines, list marker text — sits between the cursor and a content boundary.

**Why:** the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-edit-enforcement` (2026-07-21) found this as one systemic gap behind four apparently-separate symptoms: Backspace across a gap needing one keystroke per blank line, Backspace at a list item's content start eating the marker instead of merging, a selected node+its-own-subtree hitting a "Nothing to act on" veto (a related but distinct cover-math bug), and Enter-with-children jumping the remainder over the whole subtree. Trailing-gap ownership is correct for byte-fidelity **storage** but was leaking into user-facing editing semantics — a recurring theme this project's own docs/research/13 already flagged across selection enforcement and then edit enforcement.

**How to apply:** when a user gesture's *intent* is content-level (merge two nodes, split at a point) but raw edit shapes are ambiguous between "chrome edit" and "content edit" (same bytes, e.g. deleting a gap-line newline vs. deleting the separator before real content), disambiguate using the **pre-edit cursor position**, not just the diff shape. Keep a native escape hatch for deliberate chrome editing (cursor left ON a gap line/marker region). See [[node-edit-enforcement]] design.md's D9 "chrome transparency" principle and D10/D11 amendments for the concrete pattern (`recognizeMergeIntent` in `src/enforce.ts`, `cursorBefore` fact threaded from `src/plugin/transaction-filter.ts` through `src/classify.ts`).

content.text:
## Amendment round complete

Both your mechanical-bug reports and the systemic pattern are now fixed and verified:

**Bugs fixed:**
- Heading/paragraph/list-item + own-subtree selection → "Nothing to act on" veto (cover math missed the ancestor-descendant case escalate.ts already handled)
- Filed the space-indented-paragraph decoration bug separately — you already ran that fix in its own session

**Chrome-transparency amendment** (your core diagnosis, confirmed and implemented):
- New principle: gap lines and list markers are storage-layer chrome; no editing semantic may depend on them. A cursor placed on the chrome itself stays the escape hatch for deliberate whitespace editing.
- **Merges** are now cursor-derived and gap-width-independent: Backspace across *any* size gap merges in one keystroke; marker-space Backspace merges into the previous item/paragraph instead of corrupting the marker; cross-kind joins work (list item ↔ paragraph); children re-parent instead of rejecting; a heading absorbs single-line content only.
- **Split** now lands the remainder as the new first child when the node has children, instead of jumping past the whole subtree.
- All 5 delta specs amended (`node-edit-enforcement`, `structural-operations`, `transaction-classification`, plus new `outline-keyboard-grammar` and `e2e-verification` deltas), `design.md` got D9–D11.

**Verification:** 783 unit tests, full e2e suite green twice consecutively (desktop, 12/12 spec files each run) plus a clean mobile-emulation pass, lint/typecheck clean. Along the way I found and fixed one harness-only quirk (a direct-CM6-dispatch test helper didn't survive to a following keypress under mobile emulation — swapped to the more representative `setSelection` path).

**What's left:** a second real-vault pass on this amended behavior — that's yours to judge. The vault's rebuilt and ready (`test-vault/`). Let me know what you find.

okay. this is definitely an improvement and now feels more natural. but it needs more work and potentially more discussion on the fundamentals.

* first, there is a new bug: when merging two nodes (backspace at the beginning, or delete at the end), the result looks correct, but the cursor jumps to the beginning of the new merged node. it is supposed to be at the merge point, between the end of the first node and beginning of the second. 
   * related to this, I wonder if it's in scope here to also control the movement of cursor. same way as pressing backspace at the beginning of a node goes to the end of the previous one, pressing left should just move cursor to the same place, or pressing up should go to the beginning of the previous node, etc. right now we are still moving through all of the "chrome" characters that aren't part of the outline structure (which brings me to the next point)
* second point is up for a discussion. I like the new principle: "gap lines and list markers are storage-layer chrome; no editing semantic may depend on them", but I want to push back a bit on the second part: "A cursor placed on the chrome itself stays the escape hatch for deliberate whitespace editing."
   * I would go as far as saying that in the outline mode user is not supposed to interact directly with whatever isn't part of the outline (such as the "chrome"). they can always switch back to the normal markdown editing and do arbitrary character-level edits there.
   * this potentially means that cursor should just jump over the gap lines, from one node's content, to the other. and that it shouldn't be possible to place cursor outside of a node's content. no manual list dashes/bullets editing, only via structural operations.
      * this might also mean that we don't want arbitrary sized gaps, and we might even want to hide them (an idea that has already come up before). we don't need to enforce that on the text level, but it probably shouldn't be possible to create unnecessary gaps from the outline mode (i.e. if we press enter at the end of a paragraph, we insert a gap line and start a new paragraph node, but that gap is strictly necessary to separate the paragraph nodes), and hiding or normalizing gaps could be a cosmetic UX thing (similar to indentation).
   * I want your honest take on this. does it make sense or is it going to cause problems?

content.text:
Still running in the background. Let me give you my answer now while it finishes.

**The cursor bug** is fixed — `mergeNodes` was reusing `finalize`'s generic "start of the merged node" cursor convention, which is right for indent/outdent/split but wrong for a merge. It now computes the actual join position (end of `first`'s original last line, before `second`'s content got appended). Verified at the unit level (including a multi-line `first` case, so it's not just the single-line coincidence) and end-to-end through real Backspace/Delete keypresses. Full suite re-verification is running now.

**On the bigger question** — my honest take: the diagnosis is right, but I'd split the proposal into two pieces with very different risk profiles, and land only one of them now.

**List markers: yes, do it, it's the same fix pattern.** Cursor never resting inside `- ` is confined to one line, horizontal-only, and precedented (obsidian-outliner's "stick cursor to content"). It composes cleanly with what D10 already does for edits.

**Gap lines: I'd hold off, and here's the concrete reason, not just caution.** CM6's `cursorLineUp`/`cursorLineDown` track a *goal column* across consecutive vertical moves, so Down-Down-Down through lines of different lengths keeps you visually aligned. If we snap the landing position away from a gap line on every vertical move, the next move recomputes its goal column from wherever we snapped to — not from where the user's motion actually was — and that tracking will drift over a few presses. That's not hypothetical friction, it's how the mechanism works; it needs a prototype and hands-on feel-testing against real vertical navigation, not a code review. Mouse clicks on the rendered blank line have the same shape of problem: something has to decide which side of the gap wins, and "closer in pixels" vs "always next node" are both defensible until someone tries it.

There's also a real invariant to reckon with: Phase B deliberately decided cursors are never altered — it's asserted by a property test (`within-node and cursor inputs are always returned unchanged`). Extending enforcement to cursor *placement* itself, not just edits, is architecturally fine (cursor moves are transactions through the same filter — this isn't the enumerate-the-inputs anti-pattern), but it's a different invariant than what this change signed up for, and reversing it deserves its own design pass.

One thing your framing gets right that I want to affirm directly: **"switch to normal markdown mode for character-level chrome editing" is a better answer than an in-outline-mode escape hatch.** It's already how the plugin works (the toggle exists precisely for this), and it would let me delete the "cursor is deliberately on the gap line" disambiguation branch from `recognizeMergeIntent` entirely once cursor placement itself can't land there — real simplification, not just a rule swap.

**On hiding/normalizing gaps**: I'd keep those as two separate ideas. Visually hiding a gap (decoration-only, doesn't touch text) is low-risk and already has a home in `docs/research/12`. *Preventing* the user from typing extra blank lines is a different thing — it's auto-correcting keystrokes as they happen, which is the exact shape of surprise this project's own design philosophy warns against ("a wrong rewrite is surprising"). I'd leave gap-count enforcement out of scope even if we do the cursor work.

**My recommendation**: land marker-transparency for cursor/Home/Left now (small, same session), file gap-line cursor-transparency as its own follow-up change with a vertical-motion prototype as its first task, and don't touch gap-hiding/prevention at all right now. Want me to go ahead with the marker-cursor piece?
